_provenance.source_pdf: Coker, Jerry - Elements of the Jazz Language.pdf

systems.name: Jazz Language Elements
systems.summary: Coker's central system: jazz improvisation is a finite catalogue of ~18 named devices that account for nearly all the language of every player studied. Members are the named elements; traversal rules govern how elements chain into compound gestures and how the player moves between them in performance; modification rules govern how each element is altered to fit different chord-types, transposed across all 12 keys, and embellished or truncated in real solos. The system operates at three syntactic levels: digital patterns / 7-3 / 3-b9 / enclosures / bebop lick are 'letters and words'; sequences are 'complete thoughts' that organize the rest; harmonic generalization, CESH, quotes, chromatic substitutions, and stock licks layer over the basic vocabulary.

members.name: Change-Running (Chord Arpeggiation)
members.summary: Foundational chord-arpeggiation device with five functions including ear-placement, progression-learning, and pickup-note use.

members.name: Digital Patterns
members.summary: Baker's term: 4-8 note cells named by scale-degree numbers (1-2-3-5, 1-3-5-3, etc.).

members.name: Scalar Patterns
members.summary: Longer single-scale patterns including non-terminal / perpetual-motion forms; treat all scale tones equally.

members.name: 7-3 Resolution
members.summary: b7-to-3 half-step voice-leading between successive chords, most often II-7 to V7.

members.name: 3-b9
members.summary: Bidirectional melodic motion between the 3rd and flatted 9th of a dominant 7 chord, by leap or scalar fill.

members.name: Bebop Scale
members.summary: 8-note scale (major, dorian, or mixolydian) with one designated chromatic note added to fit 4/4 phrasing.

members.name: Bebop Lick
members.summary: Short scale-derived phrase on dominant 7 or minor 7 chords, woven into longer linear material.

members.name: Harmonic Generalization
members.summary: One scale covers two or more chords of a progression; ordered from safest (tonic major over II-V-I) to advanced (diminished over minor II-V).

members.name: Enclosure
members.summary: Upper-leading-tone + lower-leading-tone + object-tone formula, often embellished beyond three notes.

members.name: Sequence
members.summary: A motive followed by variations; the syntactic 'sentence' level of the language.

members.name: CESH (Contrapuntal Elaboration of Static Harmony)
members.summary: Single chord held; one chord member in motion; four varieties (minor/major × root/fifth in motion).

members.name: Quotes
members.summary: Phrases from known tunes deployed when the harmonic setting matches; three sub-varieties (external tunes, current melody, recorded solos).

members.name: Cry Me A River Lick
members.summary: Named stock lick fitting five chord-types unaltered by starting on different scale degrees.

members.name: Gone But Not Forgotten Lick
members.summary: Companion named stock lick with the same five-chord-type versatility.

members.name: Linear Chromaticism
members.summary: Non-harmonic chromatic notes added for metric fit, parallel to the bebop-scale rationale.

members.name: Tri-Tone Substitution / Altered Dominant
members.summary: Replacing a dominant 7 with another a tritone away; structurally near-identical to the altered dominant chord.

members.name: Back Door Substitution
members.summary: IV-7 to bVII7 used in place of V7; sounds correct because the ear hears the familiar precedent.

members.name: #II°7 Substitution
members.summary: #II°7 used in place of V7 by analogy with the back-door progression.

members.name: Bar-Line Shift
members.summary: Improviser arrives at a chord early or late, either accidentally (generalization, breath-pause) or deliberately (tension device).

members.name: Side-Slipping / Outside Playing
members.summary: Deliberate out-of-key playing using the five 'wrong' notes of a chord (12 chromatic minus 7 scale tones).

traversal_rules.name: Element Overlap via Shared Pivot Note
traversal_rules.summary: Elements chain when one element's terminal note serves as the next element's starting note — e.g., the 3 ending a 7-3 resolution begins a 3-b9.

references.quote_excerpt: the two devices to overlap, with the 3rd serving as both the resolution tone
elements of the jazz language carry the potential for being quickly successive, overlapping

traversal_rules.name: Sequence as Syntactic Organizer
traversal_rules.summary: Sequences organize lower-level elements into 'complete thoughts and chains of thought'; the listener perceives and predicts them, providing the communicative axis of the language.

references.quote_excerpt: sequences are more like complete thoughts, sentences, and chains of thought

traversal_rules.name: V7 Substitution Lattice
traversal_rules.summary: A V7 chord may be replaced by its tri-tone substitute, the back-door progression (IV-7 to bVII7), or #II°7; each is heard as consonant because the listener's ear hears the familiar precedent of each substitute.

references.quote_excerpt: substituting an F#7 for a C7

references.topic: Back door progression as V7 substitute
references.quote_excerpt: I chord, in a given progression, is often preceded by IV-7 to bVII7
#II°7 is often used to precede the I chord

references.topic: Why substitutions sound consonant against V7
references.quote_excerpt: listener's ear hears the given precedents for the event

traversal_rules.name: Harmonic-Generalization Ladder
traversal_rules.summary: A single scale covers multiple chords, ordered from collision-free (tonic major over II-V-I, pentatonic over ten chords) through progression-justified collision-tone cases (harmonic minor over minor II-V-I, diminished over minor II-V).

references.quote_excerpt: one scale to accommodate two or more chords of a progression
C pentatonic scale (C, D, E, G, A) will fit
three considerations that make it a modified success

modification_rules.name: Digital (Scale-Degree) Universalization
modification_rules.summary: All exercises and devices are practiced and thought of as scale-degree numbers rather than note names so they transpose instantly to all 12 keys.

references.quote_excerpt: think of it as 1-3-5-1, not C-E-G-C
Always analyze each given exercise in terms of its digital relationship

modification_rules.name: Chord-Quality Alteration of Patterns
modification_rules.summary: Any digital pattern is altered to fit a different chord-type by adjusting the affected scale degrees (1-3-5-3 to 1-b3-5-b3 for minor, etc.).

references.quote_excerpt: 1-3-5-3 pattern can be adjusted to 1-b3-5-b3 to accommodate a minor chord

modification_rules.name: Tertian Recontextualization
modification_rules.summary: The same tertian pattern serves multiple chord types depending on which scale degree it is heard from — e.g., a 1-3-5-1 in C major is 3-5-7-3 of A-7 or 5-7-9-5 of an F chord.

references.quote_excerpt: first four notes of #12 (C-E-G-C) are taken to be 1-3-5-1 in C major

modification_rules.name: Retrograde and Fifth-Rooting
modification_rules.summary: Generative transformations that multiply vocabulary: a pattern is reversed (1-3-2-1 from 1-2-3-1) or placed on the fifth of the chord (5-6-7-9 from 1-2-3-5).

references.quote_excerpt: used in their retrograde (backward) form

modification_rules.name: Bebop-Scale Chromatic Insertion
modification_rules.summary: Add one specific chromatic passing note to the major (between 5-6), dorian (between 3-4), or mixolydian (between 7-8) scale so an 8-note cell fits four beats of eighth notes and places chord tones on downbeats.

references.quote_excerpt: common scale (major, dorian, or mixolydian, usually) that has one specific chromatic tone

references.topic: Added Chromatic Note by Scale Type
references.quote_excerpt: added chromatic note in the major scale is the half-step between the fifth and sixth
eight-note scale, instead of a seven-note scale, in order to fit a 4/4 time signature

modification_rules.name: Embellishment and Truncation of Elements
modification_rules.summary: Elements appear in disguised, decorated, or incomplete forms in real solos — enclosures often extend past three notes, CESH may be truncated or heavily embellished — and the analyst must recognize them despite the disguise.

references.quote_excerpt: Enclosures, however, are often decorated/embellished
incomplete CESH; that is, play a CESH, but not to the full extension

modification_rules.name: Outside-Playing Wrong-Note Arithmetic
modification_rules.summary: Twelve chromatic notes minus the seven scale tones leaves five 'wrong' notes per chord; outside playing uses these in convincing groups or sparingly with smooth voice-leading.

references.quote_excerpt: twelve notes in the chromatic scale, simple subtraction reveals that there are five 'wrong' notes

systems.name: Four-Stage Element Assimilation Method
systems.summary: Coker's master practice methodology, made explicit in Appendix B: every element must pass through (1) mental understanding via definitions and illustrations, (2) hearing the element in master solos, (3) hands-on practice in all 12 keys, tempos and registers, (4) ear-to-harmony association through play-along exercise tracks, and (5) application to real tune progressions. A local three-stage progression (alone → exercise track → play-along tune) is applied to every device.

members.name: Mental Understanding
members.summary: Definitions and illustrations.

members.name: Aural Observation of Masters
members.summary: Hear the element in solos by masters of the craft.

members.name: Hands-On Execution
members.summary: Practice the element and its variations at different tempos, all keys, all registers.

members.name: Ear-Harmony Association
members.summary: Play element against play-along exercise tracks until sound-of-element binds to sound-of-harmony.

members.name: Application to Real Tune Progressions
members.summary: Discern locations within real tune progressions where each element can be deployed.

traversal_rules.name: Sequential Stage Progression
traversal_rules.summary: Stages are sequential; intellectual understanding is necessary but insufficient, and physical/aural habit must be built before an element appears in performance.

references.quote_excerpt: The absorption of an element happens in stages
Merely reading the book, even with the deepest understanding, will not reap

traversal_rules.name: Local Three-Stage Practice Progression
traversal_rules.summary: Within Stage 3-4, every device is practiced alone, then with an exercise track that accommodates its chord-type, then with a play-along tune whose progression offers many opportunities for it.

references.quote_excerpt: first be practiced alone, then with an exercise track

modification_rules.name: All-Twelve-Keys Discipline
modification_rules.summary: Every exercise is practiced in 12 keys with the appropriate accompaniment track; key motion follows cycle-of-fifths and chromatic sequences that mirror real-tune harmonic motion.

references.quote_excerpt: All exercises should be practiced in 12 keys and with the appropriate accompaniment
Chord roots most often move around the circle of keys

modification_rules.name: Wean from Notation
modification_rules.summary: Move quickly from notated/cycle-illustration practice to mental and aural figuring; the target skill is improvisation, not reading.

references.quote_excerpt: wean yourself away from both the notated form and the cycle

modification_rules.name: Four-Transposition / Five-Extension Regimen
modification_rules.summary: Each chord-type is arpeggiated through four transposition sequences (chromatic up/down, cycle of fifths, minor thirds, whole steps) and five extension levels (triad through 13th).

references.quote_excerpt: modulating up and down in half-steps (chromatic sequence), around the cycle of fifths
